title: I Flew to Europe to See If Their Food Is Actually Better

chunks.title: Introduction to European Diet
chunks.summary: The speaker sets the stage for their journey to Europe, specifically Spain, aiming to explore the differences in food quality compared to the United States. They express skepticism about the generally believed health advantages of European diets over American diets, particularly concerning the toxic ingredients in US food.
chunks.transcript: We are going to Europe. We're going to Spain. [Music] I have heard that in Europe the food is healthier and that in Europe no one is obese. No one is fat. No one is sick. I don't know if that's true, but I've heard that in Europe everything is different with the food. So I'm going to go to Europe and figure out what's different about the food in Europe versus the food in the States because obviously in the US we are super unhealthy and our food here is super toxic and we hear everyone talking about all of these horrible ingredients in our food that have gotten through the the grass standards, the generally recognized the safe standards from the FDA and all this bull So going to go to some markets in Europe. Going to go find some cheese and some raw milk in Europe and I'm going to go see if people are actually healthier in Europe or if it's all a scam. I'm flying to Mallorca.

chunks.title: Food Choices During Travel
chunks.summary: While preparing for their flight, the speaker discusses the specific foods they packed for the journey, emphasizing the importance of bringing healthy options. They critique the unhealthy food choices available at American airports, vowing not to partake in them during travel.
chunks.transcript: might as well go somewhere beautiful and blue water. My circadian rhythm is about to get completely destroyed, but I'm going to fix that too. And I've got a bunch of food for the flight. Let me show you guys what I got for this flight. I have squash and cucumber. It looks gross. I got peaches. I got plums, meat that I cooked yesterday. That is honeydew melon in glass. We've got a huge thing of berries and of course we've got lineage meat sticks. That's about it. Super important hack is that when you have a bag and it's all food like this it doesn't count as a carry-on bag. So I could actually bring two bags with me on this plane and this. I'm not going to eat anything on this plane.

chunks.title: Comparison of Dietary Effects
chunks.summary: The speaker compares dietary experiences, discussing lower obesity rates in Spain versus the USA and the perceived quality of food. They delve into the prevalence of diabetes, questioning the connection between obesity and dietary choices in both countries.
chunks.transcript: and raw cheese. One of the things that's interesting that I figured out doing some research is that the obesity rate in Spain is much lower than the United States. United States, we are super super fat. 42% obesity in the United States. Across Europe, rates of obesity are much lower. In Spain, it's like 19% obesity. So, like half what we have in the states. But the rates of diabetes in Spain are the same as what we have in states. And I think that the rates of diabetes in the US and in Spain are underestimated because the diagnosis is is lagging. I think a lot of people that are obese probably have some degree of pre-diabetes. But Spain is 10 to 15% diabetes. In the US, it's kind of about the same. So, even though we're much more fat in the US, the rate of diabetes is the same in Spain. Which is just to say that there's plenty of garbage food even in Spain, on Majorca, in Europe. And I see that in grocery stores. I see plenty of processed sugar. I see seed oils. I see less additives. I see less stuff that's like weird ingredients that you're not aware of, things that are probably an issue in the United States, food dyes, this kind of stuff. But there's still plenty of garbage food. And if you go to the grocery store in Spain, even the nicer grocery stores, there's plenty of soda and garbage garbage food. So, it's not surprising. chunks.title: Final Thoughts and Conclusions
chunks.summary: As the trip concludes, the speaker summarizes their findings on food quality in Mallorca compared to the US, noting significant differences in additives and sourcing. They emphasize the potential for replicating the European experience in the US through intentional food choices.
chunks.transcript: [Music] The trip in Majorca is coming to an end. And I wanted to kind of summarize some of the things that I've learned here about food in Majorca. I don't know if this is representative of the food everywhere in Europe, but this was my first shot at this question. I think this question gets asked a lot. Why do I feel better in Europe when I'm eating? Why do I feel better in Spain or Italy or France than I do in the United States? Why can I eat pizza or pasta or bread in these countries and feel fine or desserts, pastries, and not feel great in the United States when I do those things. What I've noticed about being here is that overall, at least in Majorca, and I explored a lot of this island over the last 2 weeks, the food quality here is far and away better than the United States in general. I went to little corner grocery stores, not so great. We saw that at the beginning of the video. I went to bigger grocery stores, which had all kinds of stuff. That ham was incredible. And then there were little specialty shops that had more of the organic stuff, which they call eco or bio. On this little island, I guess this is a self-sustainable agricultural hub. There's plenty of organic produce. Organic peaches are in season now. I got a lot of organic melons, but there definitely were a lot of local vegetables. I saw lettuce and cucumbers, which I ate. There were avocados. I think it's all grown on the island. Um, or a lot of it is at least. And when I went to the market in the little town near here, Soller, that was really cool cuz that farmer had driven maybe an hour with the stuff from his farm and he had everything. He had so much stuff. That part of the trip was probably one of my favorite parts. It was just so cool to see all the stuff that he brought. All the dialects here are a little tricky because I speak Spanish. Being in Costa Rica, my Spanish isn't perfect. Formatajes. But you get um, you get Catalan here and then you get Majorcan, which is even different than Catalan. So, sometimes it's hard to understand people, but I do my best. But that was really cool to meet him and the peaches that I had from him were out of this world. So, the food quality here is very good. And I think that a lot of the foods that people like to eat as junk foods in the States are probably a little bit better here because of the flowers. I think that a lot of the local municipalities are not using glyphosate, though glyphosate is still legal in Spain at least. And I think that the wheat is a different type of wheat here. It's a different heritage wheat. It's an older type of wheat. So, maybe that's a big deal. I think there there are less preservatives here, although there are still some. If you look at ingredient labels from Spain or from Europe in general versus the US, there definitely are less things allowed here in Europe. I've talked about this in the past. If you look at the list of ingredients in the United States that have been introduced into our food supply through the GRAS the GRAS standards, it's 5 to 6,000, maybe 7,000. It's only 4 to 500 in Europe. So, there are a lot of chemicals that are used in our food in the States as preservatives, as fillers, as gums, as binders. I think that makes a big difference. Even when you're looking at kind of like junk foods like cookies or something. The meat here was incredible, generally speaking. I got the steak at the grocery store and it was like orange. I mean, that the fat was orange. So, this is at this local grocery store called Veritas, which is like their boutique organic grocery store, but you don't see a lot of meat in the States even grass-fed meat with fat that's that orange. That is really suggestive that these cows are eating a lot of beta-carotene in the grass. One thing to mention about the eggs in Europe or at least in Majorca and Spain, I think it's most of Europe, they don't refrigerate them here because the governments don't allow them to clean off the protective coating of the eggs. When you refrigerate eggs and you pull them in and out of the fridge, that can cause condensation. And if that protective coating on the shell has been removed, that can cause bacteria, potentially salmonella or campylobacter, to transit into the egg from the shell. Generally, eggs should be sterile. So, if you don't do that, if you leave the coating on the egg, your egg doesn't need to be refrigerated. It's crazy to see it here, but they're not even refrigerated. And that is apparently lowering the risk of foodborne illness because of the bacterial transit through the eggshell. And you can get organic eggs or like their bio eco eggs, but the eggs that I got at that farmer's market from that local farmer who had brought them, you guys saw me talk to that guy and he said, "I don't know. He just brings us the eggs." Those were some of the most orange yolks I've seen. And I don't think this is like the States where they're feeding them, you know, funky marigold flowers to make the yolks fake orange. So, those were really high quality eggs. So, I mean, none of this is stuff you couldn't figure out in the States, right? If you had local farmers in the US and you went to your local farmers, you could get things like you can here in Europe. I think if you're eating if you want to eat bread, if you feel good with bread, I don't, but if you do, ferment the bread like a sourdough and use a heritage wheat like a an einkorn wheat and get something that's organic, you might be able to duplicate what's going on in Europe and use foods with less ingredients without these funky ingredients. I always think of this rule like if your great grandmother wouldn't recognize an ingredient on that label, why are you eating it? So that's the great grandmother rule. If it fails the great grandmother rule, don't eat it guys. Don't eat it. It's horrible. So I think if you do that, you're getting closer to food in Europe in general, less additives.
